He is often considered to be the first philosopher in the Greek tradition. He attempted to explain the natural world without reference to mythology and instead relied on his own observations and reasoning, earning him the title of "the father of Western philosophy". He is also recognized as one of the Seven Sages of Greece. He is often cited as the first person to have demonstrated that magnets can attract iron and to have understood that friction generates static electricity. He is also credited with the first use of deductive reasoning applied to geometry, by deriving four corollaries to Thales' theorem.
